behaviors.description: Renamed files appear in their own category and are not also counted as a deletion plus an addition.
behaviors.justification: The likely real failure is at the git subprocess boundary (e.g., missing/incorrect rename-detection flags causing git to emit add+delete instead of rename). A mocked git response could hide that. Behavior text is about observed CLI output from a real repository, so real git + filesystem interaction is the minimum reliable boundary. Higher levels are disqualified: no AI/ML call or multi-agent pipeline is present in the behavior.
behaviors.test_description: Create a temporary real git repo, commit a file, rename it, run the CLI via subprocess against the repo, and assert stdout report includes the file only in `renamed` and not in both `added` and `deleted`.

behaviors.description: Diffs that include binary files do not cause the tool to crash; binary files are counted in the output.
behaviors.justification: Binary-diff handling failures commonly arise from real git output/encoding/subprocess behavior and real filesystem binary content. In-process fakes can miss decode/exit-code/format edge cases. The behavior explicitly concerns running the tool without crashing on real binary diffs, which crosses OS/filesystem/subprocess boundaries. Higher levels are disqualified because no model output quality is involved.
behaviors.test_description: Initialize a real git repo, add and change a binary file (e.g., random bytes), run the CLI as a subprocess, assert zero/non-crash execution and that binary file changes are counted in the structured stdout report.

behaviors.description: Running the tool on a repository that has no commits yet produces an empty report rather than an error.
behaviors.justification: `No commits yet` is a specific git state with real command semantics and exit behavior (unborn HEAD), which is subprocess/OS-visible behavior. Mocking repository history can hide failures tied to actual git error messages/exit codes. Behavior text requires executing the CLI against a real repo state. Higher levels are disqualified: no AI/ML dependency or multi-step agent chain.
behaviors.test_description: Create an empty initialized git repo with zero commits, invoke the CLI subprocess, assert it exits successfully and prints an empty structured report (not an error).

behaviors.description: The counts in the report match the actual change history of the repository being analyzed.
behaviors.justification: This validates end-to-end agreement with real git history traversal, not just internal counting logic. Real failures can come from command selection, revision ranges, and git subprocess semantics that mocks may not reproduce. The behavior explicitly compares CLI output to actual repository history, so real repo + real git subprocess is the minimum. Agentic/Workflow are disqualified by absence of AI/ML steps.
behaviors.test_description: Construct a real git repo with a known sequence of commits containing added/deleted/renamed/modified files, run the CLI subprocess over that history window, and assert reported category counts exactly match the known ground truth.

behaviors.description: The output is identical whether the changes being analyzed are staged or unstaged.
behaviors.justification: Staged vs unstaged equivalence depends on real git index/working-tree semantics and command behavior. In-process stubs can hide discrepancies in how the CLI queries git state. The behavior is specifically about CLI output under real repository states, requiring filesystem + git subprocess interaction. Higher levels are disqualified because there is no model decision-making.
behaviors.test_description: In a real temp git repo, prepare identical content changes in two scenarios (staged and unstaged), run the CLI subprocess for each scenario, and assert the structured stdout outputs are identical.
